questions.question: Compare direct-mapped, fully associative, and set-associative cache mapping techniques.
keyPoints: Direct-mapped: One location per block, simple, conflict misses
Fully associative: Any location, no conflicts, expensive
Set-associative: Compromise, n locations per set
Include diagrams for each type
Trade-offs: Simplicity vs flexibility vs cost

questions.question: A 32KB, 4-way set-associative cache has 64-byte lines. For a 32-bit address, calculate the number of sets and the bit fields (tag, index, offset).
keyPoints: Total lines = 32KB / 64B = 512 lines
Sets = 512 / 4 = 128 sets
Offset bits = log2(64) = 6 bits
Index bits = log2(128) = 7 bits
Tag bits = 32 - 6 - 7 = 19 bits
questions.modelAnswer: Lines = 32KB/64B = 512. Sets = 512/4 = 128. Offset = log2(64) = 6 bits. Index = log2(128) = 7 bits. Tag = 32-6-7 = 19 bits.

questions.question: Explain write-through and write-back cache policies with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Write-through: Write to cache and memory simultaneously
Advantages: Memory always consistent, simple recovery
Disadvantages: Slower writes, higher memory traffic
Write-back: Write only to cache, update memory on eviction
Advantages: Faster, less traffic
Disadvantages: Need dirty bit, memory may be stale

questions.question: Explain the 3 Cs of cache misses. How can each type be reduced?
keyPoints: Compulsory: First access to block, reduce with prefetching
Capacity: Cache too small, reduce with larger cache
Conflict: Limited associativity, reduce with higher associativity
Also mention victim cache for conflict misses
Larger blocks help spatial locality but can hurt

questions.question: Calculate AMAT for a system with L1 cache (1ns, 5% miss), L2 cache (10ns, 10% miss), and memory (100ns).
keyPoints: L1 hit contribution: 0.95 × 1 = 0.95 ns
L2 hit contribution: 0.05 × 0.90 × (1+10) = 0.495 ns
Memory contribution: 0.05 × 0.10 × (1+10+100) = 0.555 ns
